message:
perf(indexer): switch lexical-rebuild fingerprint to content-based (content-v1) + parallelize cass document construction

Two coupled changes that both target the lexical-rebuild path's
correctness/perf trade-offs.

src/indexer/mod.rs — content-based fingerprint
----------------------------------------------
The prior `lexical_rebuild_storage_fingerprint()` derived its
identity from the database file's metadata stamp:
  `<db_len>:<db_mtime_ms>:<wal_len>:<wal_mtime_ms>`

That worked when there was exactly one writer producing strictly-
appending WAL output, but it produced spurious "rebuild needed"
signals in two real situations:

  1. After a vacuum or schema migration, the file size and mtime
     change without any user-visible content change — the prior
     fingerprint flipped, triggering a full rebuild.

  2. After a rsync/cp transfer, the destination's mtime usually
     differs from the source's even though the content is byte-
     identical — same false positive.

New helper `lexical_rebuild_content_fingerprint(storage,
total_conversations)` derives identity from the content state:
  `content-v1:<total_conversations>:<MAX(conversations.id)>:<MAX(messages.id)>`

Identical content → identical fingerprint regardless of file-
system metadata.  The "v1" prefix is load-bearing — see below.

`lexical_rebuild_storage_fingerprint(db_path)` now opens the DB
read-only, calls `count_total_conversations_exact`, computes the
content fingerprint, and closes the storage best-effort.  This
adds one read-only DB open per rebuild check, which is cheap
(opens are sub-millisecond on warm pages) and is paid only on
the rare "should we rebuild?" decision path.

Backward compatibility:

  - `lexical_rebuild_db_state_matches()` (the renamed strict
    matcher) compares only `total_conversations`,
    `storage_fingerprint`, and the path; messages count is
    derivable from the fingerprint so we don't need to require
    it match independently.

  - `lexical_rebuild_db_state_matches_legacy()` keeps the prior
    looser comparison logic (treat zero-message/zero-conversation
    rows as wildcards) for state files written before the
    `content-v1:` switch.

  - `LexicalRebuildState::matches_run()` now picks between the
    two: if BOTH the saved AND the current fingerprint start with
    `content-v1:`, use the strict matcher; otherwise fall back
    to the legacy matcher.  This means a rebuild state file from
    before this commit keeps working, and the strict path turns
    on automatically once the rebuild persists a new state.

`should_evaluate_incremental_canonical_lexical_repair(context)`
extracted as a named predicate from the four-condition guard
that previously gated `choose_incremental_canonical_lexical_repair_plan`.
The condition was being repeated and is easier to read as a
named function.

src/search/tantivy.rs — parallel cass document construction
-----------------------------------------------------------
Refactored cass document building from the append-mutation form
  `fn append_cass_document_for_message(&mut Vec<FsCassDocument>,
       &mut usize, &CassDocContext, &NormalizedMessage)`
into a return-based form
  `fn cass_document_for_message(&CassDocContext,
       &NormalizedMessage) -> Option<FsCassDocument>`

Now each per-message call has no shared mutable state and can be
fanned out via rayon's `par_iter()` when the message slice is
above a small threshold.  Two new helpers wrap the choice:
`build_cass_documents_for_message_slice_serial` for small batches
(no overhead) and a parallel sibling for large batches.  The
caller's char-budget tracking is preserved by summing the
`msg.content.len()` over the produced documents on the merge
side.

Net effect: when a conversation has hundreds of messages (long-
running CLI sessions are normal), the prep step now scales with
available cores.  Smaller conversations stay on the serial path
where rayon's per-task overhead would dominate.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
